subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, and the position of equilibrium can be predicted and shifted using Le Châtelier's principle.

core_concepts.title: The Equilibrium Constant
core_concepts.detail: At equilibrium, $K_c = \frac{[\text{products}]^{\text{coeff}}}{[\text{reactants}]^{\text{coeff}}}$. Pure solids and liquids are excluded (activity = 1). If $K > 1$, products are favored; if $K < 1$, reactants are favored. $K_p$ uses partial pressures; $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n_{gas}}$. Reversing a reaction takes the reciprocal of $K$; multiplying coefficients by $n$ raises $K$ to the $n$th power.

core_concepts.title: The Reaction Quotient Q
core_concepts.detail: Q has the same form as $K$ but uses current (non-equilibrium) concentrations. If $Q < K$: reaction proceeds forward (more products needed). If $Q > K$: reaction proceeds in reverse (more reactants needed). If $Q = K$: system is at equilibrium.

core_concepts.title: ICE Tables
core_concepts.detail: ICE (Initial, Change, Equilibrium) tables organize equilibrium calculations. Set up the table with initial concentrations, represent changes using the stoichiometric ratio with variable $x$, then substitute equilibrium expressions into $K_c$. The 5% rule: if $x/[\text{initial}] < 5\%$, the approximation $[\text{HA}] - x \approx [\text{HA}]$ is valid.

core_concepts.title: Le Châtelier's Principle
core_concepts.detail: When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to counteract the change. Adding reactant: shifts right. Removing product: shifts right. Increasing pressure (decreasing volume): shifts toward fewer moles of gas. Increasing temperature: shifts toward the endothermic direction (and changes $K$). Adding an inert gas at constant volume: no shift. Catalyst: speeds up both directions equally; no shift.

core_concepts.title: Solubility Equilibria (Ksp)
core_concepts.detail: For slightly soluble salt $\text{MX}(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{M}^+(aq) + \text{X}^-(aq)$: $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^+][\text{X}^-]$. Molar solubility $s$ is related to $K_{sp}$ through the stoichiometry of dissolution. The common-ion effect: adding a common ion decreases solubility by pushing the equilibrium left (Q > K → precipitate forms).

core_concepts.title: Temperature Dependence of K
core_concepts.detail: Temperature is the only factor that changes the value of $K$ (unlike concentration, pressure, or catalyst, which only shift position). For exothermic reactions, increasing T decreases K (shifts left). For endothermic reactions, increasing T increases K (shifts right). This is why industrial processes balance yield (low T) vs. rate (high T).

formulas.name: Equilibrium constant expression
formulas.description: For the general reaction $a\text{A} + b\text{B} \rightleftharpoons c\text{C} + d\text{D}$; products over reactants, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient; pure solids/liquids omitted.

formulas.name: Kp–Kc relationship
formulas.description: Relates the pressure-based and concentration-based equilibrium constants; $\Delta n_{gas}$ = moles of gaseous products − moles of gaseous reactants.

formulas.name: Molar solubility from Ksp (1:1 salt)
formulas.description: For a 1:1 salt $\text{MX} \rightleftharpoons \text{M}^+ + \text{X}^-$: $K_{sp} = s \cdot s = s^2$; molar solubility $s = \sqrt{K_{sp}}$.

exam_tip: Temperature is the only perturbation that changes K; all other changes (concentration, pressure, catalyst) shift the equilibrium position without changing K. On the AP exam, always distinguish between a shift in equilibrium position (Q ≠ K → system responds) and a change in K (only temperature does this).
